facts: The kingdom of Kush, located south of Egypt along the Nile River, grew wealthy by trading gold and other goods with Egypt, not by crossing the Sahara Desert.

The kingdom of Ghana in West Africa (in what is now southeastern Mauritania and western Mali, not the modern country of Ghana) became powerful by taxing the trade of gold from the south and salt from the north across the Sahara.
The kingdom of Mali succeeded Ghana and grew even richer because its rulers controlled both gold mines and salt mines, allowing them to dominate trans-Saharan trade.
Salt was as valuable as gold in medieval West Africa because people needed it to preserve food and stay healthy, and it was scarce in the southern rainforests.
The Songhai kingdom became the largest empire in West Africa after Mali by controlling trade routes along the Niger River and across the Sahara Desert.
misconceptions: Students think the ancient kingdom of Ghana is the same as the modern country of Ghana, but they are different because ancient Ghana was located farther north in the Sahel region.
Students believe salt was not an important trade good because it is cheap today, but in medieval West Africa salt was as valuable as gold because it was scarce and essential for survival.
